B) \(\Delta E = \Delta H = 29.718 \, \text{kJ}\), \(q = - 29.718 \, \text0\right] The correct answer is B). In a reversible, adiabatic process for an ideal gas where no heat exchange occurs (\(q=0\) and \(\Delta H=0\) since there's no change in enthalpy), the work done on the system (compression) results in energy being stored within it. This is reflected as an increase in internal energy, hence \( \Delta E = 29.718 \, \text{kJ}\). The negative sign for q indicates that this process requires input of energy to compress the gas against its natural tendency (work done on system), which aligns with our understanding of work and heat transfer principles in thermodynamics.

Brown v. Board of Education, as referenced in the provided information from a Supreme Court case decision that declared state laws establishing separate public schools for black and white students to be unconstitutional. This landmark ruling was indeed part of broader civil rights movements which sought desegregation across various sectors including education following promises made during Reconstruction era after the Civil War, wherein African Americans were promised equal protection under law but faced systemic discrimination and segregation instead.

D) Both statements are partially true. While research has shown a correlation between wealth and life satisfaction to some extent (though this relationship can vary greatly among different societies), it's not universally proven that as countries get richer their populations report higher average life satisfaction due to factors like inequality, social comparison, etc. Regarding the second statement about important life events affecting happiness but having little long-term impact, studies have shown these events do cause short-term fluctuations in wellbeing; however, people often return to a baseline level of happiness over time (happiness set point theory).

Both statements can be true under certain conditions (e.g., when M and N are false, O is also false), which makes them not necessarily leading to the same truth values in all cases, hence they're neither logically equivalent nor contradictory; however, there exist scenarios where both could simultaneously hold true without contradiction, making them consistent as well.
